candidate_problems.text: How should 'equivalent to the true invariants' and 'isomorphic up to an information-preserving transformation' be made precise, especially when many non-isomorphic latent decompositions induce the same predictive state? The conjecture hinges on a notion of reality-preserving equivalence, but prediction typically identifies only observational/predictive equivalence classes, not ontology. A key open problem is to characterize when predictive-state minimality uniquely selects a latent invariant structure versus merely one of many representationally interchangeable summaries.

Under what exact conditions does the minimal sufficient statistic over histories exist, remain finite or low-complexity, and coincide with the quotient of latent states by future-equivalence? The conjecture assumes a clean predictive state, but in partially observed, non-Markov, continuous, or infinite-memory systems the minimal predictor may be infinite-dimensional, non-computable, or highly unstable. Clarifying existence, complexity, and robustness of this object is crucial to knowing when the conjecture is substantive rather than vacuous.

How do optimization, architecture, and implicit regularization affect whether next-token training actually converges to invariant-bearing internal variables rather than distributed, entangled, or memorizing solutions with identical predictive performance? The conjecture separates in-principle identifiability from practical learning, but the bridge is unresolved: even if invariants are necessary for Bayes-optimal compression, real learners may realize them in non-interpretable ways or fail to realize them at all. A major open problem is to identify structural or training conditions under which invariant recovery is not just possible but likely.
